title: Grammar · English A2
scope: A2 (Cambridge Key) adds the past simple, future forms (going to / will), comparatives and superlatives, countable/uncountable nouns with quantifiers, adverbs of frequency and manner, must/have to and should, and an introduction to the present perfect. Boundary: you can talk about the past and make simple plans, but complex tenses and conditionals come later.

blocks.text: For finished actions in the past. Regular verbs add -ed; many common verbs are irregular.

rows: go → went, see → saw, have → had

blocks.text: Negatives and questions use did + base verb: I didn't go. Did you see it? (not: did you saw).
be going to + base verb expresses plans and intentions, or predictions based on evidence.
I am going to study tonight. Look at the clouds — it's going to rain.
will + base verb for predictions, offers and decisions made at the moment of speaking.
I think it will be sunny. I'll help you. Don't worry, I won't forget.
Short adjectives add -er/-est; longer ones use more/most. Some are irregular.
London is bigger than Bath. This is the best restaurant in town.
Countable nouns can be plural; uncountable cannot. Quantifiers differ accordingly.
Use some in affirmatives and any in negatives/questions: I have some money. I don't have any time.
always, usually, often, sometimes, rarely, never. They go before the main verb but after to be.
I always have breakfast. She is usually late. They never play tennis.
Both express obligation. have to is more common for external rules; mustn't means it is prohibited, don't have to means it is not necessary.
I have to work tomorrow. You mustn't smoke here. You don't have to come.
should + base verb gives advice or recommendations.
You should see a doctor. You shouldn't eat so much sugar.
have/has + past participle, for experiences and recent actions with a present link. Often with ever, never, just, already, yet.
I have visited Paris. She has just arrived. Have you finished yet?
Describe how an action is done; usually formed from an adjective + -ly.
She speaks slowly. He works hard. (irregular: good → well)
